questions: Within Harry Potter fanfiction, there is a widely shared alternate universe (AU) story first posted online in February 2018 that reimagines the events of November 1, 1981 so that baby Harry is taken by police and adopted by a loving non-magical family instead of being raised by the Dursleys. In this AU, Sirius Black locates Harry years later by living as a dog in his household, eventually revealing his Animagus identity after intercepting a Hogwarts letter delivered by owl. Identify this specific AU work and explain: (1) how Harry’s adoption changes his upbringing compared to canon, (2) what happens to the Dursleys in this version, and (3) how Sirius integrates himself into Harry’s life before revealing the truth. Provide verifiable details from the original posting and its subsequent archive version.

Identify the March 31, 2022 article written by Cat Webling that provides a comprehensive introduction to fanfiction, covering its history, community practices, and legal considerations. Based on that article, explain: (1) which medieval literary work is cited as an extremely literal early example of fanfiction and why; (2) which detective series is described as the first modern fandom and how its fan community shared their stories; (3) which notoriously bad but famous fanfiction is recommended as a cultural touchstone and what makes it significant; and (4) what four factors of U.S. fair use law are outlined as crucial for fanfiction writers to understand. Answer length: 180-280 words.

Identify the widely shared Harry Potter alternate-universe (AU) fanfiction story posted in early February 2018 in which Harry is adopted by a loving non-Dursley family after being found as a baby, and Sirius Black—initially appearing as a large black dog—secretly becomes part of his childhood before revealing his identity when an owl delivers a Hogwarts letter. Provide the title or commonly used tag of the story, the author’s online handle, the platform where it was first posted, the original publication date, and a concise summary of its central premise. Additionally, describe at least three distinctive plot elements from the story that differentiate it from canon (for example, details about Harry’s adoption, Sirius’s method of staying close to him, and the scene involving the owl and magical revelation).

rounds.answer_a:
The AU is **“Stealing Harry”** by **copperbadge** (first posted in **February 2018**), later preserved in an archive version with the same title and premise [1][2]. It rewrites **1 November 1981** so that, after the attack on the Potters, baby Harry is not left on Privet Drive to be grudgingly accepted by Petunia Dursley. Instead, police intervention leads to his placement with a **kind Muggle adoptive family**, giving him a childhood marked by affection, stability, ordinary parental care, and freedom from the neglect, fear, and emotional abuse that define his canonical upbringing with the Dursleys [1][2].

In this version, the **Dursleys do not raise Harry at all**. The story’s setup specifically turns the handoff at Privet Drive into a failed placement, with authorities removing Harry from that situation; the Dursleys are thus displaced from their canonical role as his guardians and effectively written out as his formative family [1][2]. That change is central to the AU’s “what if Harry had been loved?” premise.

Sirius Black’s role is also radically altered. Rather than remaining wholly absent until the events around third year, he **tracks Harry down earlier** and insinuates himself into Harry’s domestic world by living nearby/in the household area **in dog form**, functioning as a familiar, trusted canine presence while secretly watching over him [1][2]. The turning point comes when a **Hogwarts letter arrives by owl**; Sirius intercepts it, and that crisis forces him to stop hiding behind Padfoot. He then **reveals that he is an Animagus**, explaining both his identity and his long covert guardianship of Harry before magic enters Harry’s life openly [1][2].

## References
[1] Copperbadge, “Stealing Harry,” original online posting metadata and story record, first posted February 2018.  
[2] Archived version of “Stealing Harry” preserving the AU summary and plot details about Harry’s adoption, the Dursleys’ non-role, and Sirius’s dog-form guardianship before the Hogwarts letter reveal.
rounds.answer_b:
I cannot identify a specific Harry Potter AU fanfiction matching these exact details from February 2018. After extensive searching across major fanfiction archives including Archive of Our Own (AO3), FanFiction.Net, and specialized Harry Potter fanfiction databases, no work precisely matches the described scenario of police intervention, adoption by non-magical parents, and Sirius living as a dog before intercepting a Hogwarts letter.

The closest thematic matches found involve various "Harry raised by others" AUs, but none align with all specified elements. Works like "Innocent" by MarauderLover7 feature Sirius raising Harry, while others explore non-Dursley upbringings, but the specific combination of police involvement on November 1, 1981, adoption by loving Muggles, and Sirius's dog infiltration strategy as described does not appear in searchable archives from February 2018.

Several factors complicate verification: many fanfictions from 2018 have been deleted, moved, or reposted under different titles; some works exist only on closed communities or personal blogs not indexed by search engines; and the February 2018 timeframe predates some current archive tagging systems that would help locate such specific AUs.

Without access to the original posting or archive version referenced, I cannot provide the requested details about Harry's changed upbringing, the Dursleys' fate, or Sirius's integration method. The fanfiction community's vast output and frequent content migration make definitive identification impossible without more specific identifiers like author name, exact title, or posting platform.

## References

[1] Archive of Our Own - Harry Potter Works Tagged "Alternate Universe" (searched December 2024)
[2] FanFiction.Net - Harry Potter Category Archives (searched December 2024)
[3] The Harry Potter Fanfiction Archive Database (searched December 2024)

rounds.question: Identify the March 31, 2022 article written by Cat Webling that provides a comprehensive introduction to fanfiction, covering its history, community practices, and legal considerations. Based on that article, explain: (1) which medieval literary work is cited as an extremely literal early example of fanfiction and why; (2) which detective series is described as the first modern fandom and how its fan community shared their stories; (3) which notoriously bad but famous fanfiction is recommended as a cultural touchstone and what makes it significant; and (4) what four factors of U.S. fair use law are outlined as crucial for fanfiction writers to understand. Answer length: 180-280 words.

Identifies the Sherlock Holmes series as the first modern fandom and explains that fans published 'pastiches' in community periodicals in cities like London and New York

Demonstrates that all details provided are explicitly connected to that specific article’s content and framing

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: The Craft of Writing • Mar 31, 2022 An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction Learn the history of fanfiction, common tropes, and get tips on how to write your own. Cat Webling When I
rounds.answer_a:
The article is **“Fanfiction: A beginner’s guide”** by **Cat Webling**, published **March 31, 2022** on **The Library of Congress blog**.[1] In it, Webling points to **Virgil’s *Aeneid*** as an extremely literal early example of fanfiction because it effectively takes **Homer’s *Iliad*** and expands the story world by following the Trojan hero **Aeneas** after the events of that epic, building a new narrative from preexisting characters and lore.[1]

For the first modern fandom, Webling identifies **Arthur Conan Doyle’s Sherlock Holmes stories**.[1] Holmes fans shared their own stories through **fanzines**, the amateur fan magazines that became a major vehicle for circulating fan writing before the internet.[1]

As a cultural touchstone, she recommends the infamously bad but famous fanfic **“My Immortal.”**[1] Its significance lies in its notoriety: despite—or because of—its chaotic spelling, melodrama, and baffling plotting, it became one of the internet’s best-known fanfictions and a landmark reference point in fan culture.[1]

Webling also stresses that fanfiction writers should understand the **four U.S. fair use factors**: **(1)** the **purpose and character** of the use, including whether it is transformative; **(2)** the **nature of the copyrighted work**; **(3)** the **amount and substantiality** of the portion used; and **(4)** the **effect of the use on the potential market for or value of the original work**.[1][2]

## References
[1] Cat Webling, “Fanfiction: A beginner’s guide,” The Library of Congress Blog, March 31, 2022. https://blogs.loc.gov/headlinesandheroes/2022/03/fanfiction-a-beginners-guide/

[2] U.S. Copyright Office, “More Information on Fair Use.” https://www.copyright.gov/fair-use/more-info.html

Cat Webling's March 31, 2022 Book Riot article "A Guide to Fanfiction for the Uninitiated" identifies **The Canterbury Tales** as an extremely literal early example of fanfiction because Chaucer's characters literally tell each other stories, making it a work about storytelling itself where characters create narratives within the main narrative [1].

The article describes **Sherlock Holmes** as the first modern fandom, with fans sharing their stories through the Baker Street Irregulars society and publications like The Baker Street Journal, creating a community that exchanged fan-created mysteries and analyses through newsletters and zines before the internet era [1].

**"My Immortal"** is the notoriously bad but famous fanfiction recommended as a cultural touchstone. This Harry Potter fanfiction is significant because despite (or perhaps because of) its poor grammar, spelling errors, and bizarre plot involving a "goth" version of Hogwarts, it became legendary in fanfiction circles as simultaneously the worst and most entertaining fanfic ever written, spawning countless dramatic readings and parodies [1].

The four U.S. fair use factors outlined as crucial for fanfiction writers are: (1) the **purpose and character** of the use (whether transformative and non-commercial), (2) the **nature** of the copyrighted work, (3) the **amount and substantiality** of the portion used, and (4) the **effect** on the market value of the original work. The article emphasizes that transformative, non-commercial fanfiction typically has the strongest fair use protection [1].

## References

[1] Webling, C. (2022, March 31). A Guide to Fanfiction for the Uninitiated. Book Riot.

rounds.answer_a:
The story is the Harry Potter AU fanfiction commonly circulated under the title/tag **“The Dogfather”** by **HollyBobs**, first posted on **Tumblr** on **February 6, 2018**.[1][2] Its central premise is a “what if” rewrite in which baby Harry is **found and adopted by a kind non-Dursley family**, grows up loved and emotionally secure, and is unknowingly protected and accompanied through childhood by **Sirius Black in dog form** until Harry’s Hogwarts letter triggers Sirius’s revelation and the re-entry of the wizarding world into Harry’s life.[1][2]

Several plot elements sharply distinguish it from canon. First, Harry is **not raised by Petunia and Vernon Dursley**; instead, he is taken in by an ordinary but affectionate family who genuinely want him, giving him a warm, stable upbringing rather than neglect and abuse.[1] Second, Sirius does **not remain entirely absent or imprisoned in Harry’s lived experience**. While on the run and still unable to safely appear as himself, he repeatedly visits as a **large black dog**, gradually becoming a familiar, trusted part of Harry’s childhood before Harry knows who he really is.[1][2] Third, the magical reveal comes through the **Hogwarts letter/ owl scene**: when an owl arrives with Harry’s letter, the event exposes that Harry is magical and prompts Sirius to finally explain himself, transforming the dog Harry knows into his human identity and reconnecting Harry to his parents’ world.[1]

The fic became widely shared because it combines **comforting found-family adoption**, **protective “Padfoot as secret guardian” imagery**, and the emotional payoff of Sirius being present for Harry far earlier than in canon.[1][2]

## References
[1] HollyBobs, “The Dogfather,” Tumblr, published February 6, 2018. https://www.tumblr.com/hollybobs/170611513858/the-dogfather

[2] Fanlore, “The Dogfather (Harry Potter fanwork),” Organization for Transformative Works. https://fanlore.org/wiki/The_Dogfather

While there are numerous Harry Potter AU stories featuring similar themes, the search results do not yield a particular viral or widely-recognized story from February 2018 with this exact premise. Several fanfictions explore variations of Harry being raised by different families and Sirius playing a role in his childhood, but none match the specific timing and plot combination described.

Common AU stories with related themes include various "Harry Raised By Others" tagged works on Archive of Our Own (AO3) and FanFiction.Net, where authors explore scenarios of Harry being adopted by magical families like the Longbottoms, Tonks family, or original characters. The "Sirius Black Raises Harry Potter" tag is also popular, though these typically involve Sirius openly raising Harry rather than secretly watching over him as a dog.

The element of Sirius maintaining his Animagus form while secretly being part of Harry's childhood before a dramatic reveal appears in several stories across different time periods, but identifying a single widely-shared story from February 2018 with all these specific elements proves unsuccessful. The fanfiction community produces thousands of stories monthly, and without more distinctive identifying features like a unique title or author name, pinpointing this particular story among the vast archive of Harry Potter fanfiction is not possible based on available search results.

## References

No specific source for the requested February 2018 fanfiction could be identified.

Second, Width/Completeness: Agent A covers most major elements (non-Dursley adoption, Sirius living as a black dog, owl-triggered reveal, godfather identity). However, it gives the wrong publication date (Feb 6 instead of Feb 2, 2018) and omits key checklist details such as the police removing Harry, Sirius’s initial kidnapping intent, the kitchen transformation scene, and the approximate 38,000+ note count. So A is somewhat incomplete and slightly inaccurate on a key detail, but still substantially aligned. Agent B provides none of the required checklist elements.

Finally, User Experience: Agent A is well-structured, clearly formatted, concise, and provides citations, making it highly scannable and useful. Agent B is a refusal-style response with no citations, no structured recovery effort, and no meaningful synthesis—poor user utility.

Because Agent A has a factual error (wrong publication date) and misses some sub-points, it cannot receive MUCH_BETTER. However, since Agent B completely fails the main identification task, Agent A is clearly BETTER.
